sound_type: sustained ka-ka-ka assault -- rapid staccato alarm call fired in continuous bursts for 5-15 minutes during nest defense; fundamental frequency 800-2000 Hz; carries 200-400 meters through dense conifer forest; escalates to physical diving attack with talon contact; most aggressive nest defense of any North American raptor
ecosystem: Nest defense -- dense old-growth and mature second-growth conifer forest; nesting territory with active nest; requires large territories (1,500-3,000+ acres) of structurally complex forest
season: Resident (year-round in suitable Pacific Northwest habitat; alarm behavior most intense April-August during nesting season; some altitudinal migration in winter)
conservation_status: Least Concern (IUCN). Pacific Northwest populations dependent on old-growth and mature forest availability. Federal Late-Successional Reserves (Northwest Forest Plan 1994) provide primary habitat protection. Old-growth indicator species.
milestone: Third Accipitridae entry in SPS engine. First Accipiter genus documented (degrees 221 and 297 documented Haliaeetus). FIRST ACCIPITER IN ENGINE. Old-growth indicator species. Most aggressive nest defense of any North American raptor. Opens second consecutive Accipitridae entry at 297-298.

coordinates.description: Pacific Northwest breeding distribution: Cascades (primary), Olympics, Blue Mountains, Okanogan Highlands. Requires old-growth and mature conifer forest. Cascades breeding confirmed at multiple sites in national forests and national parks. Resident year-round in suitable habitat.

acoustic_data.vocalization_type: Ka-ka-ka alarm -- rapid staccato notes delivered in continuous bursts. Harsher and more insistent than any other PNW raptor alarm. Not modulated -- sustained at full intensity for duration of threat.
acoustic_data.frequency_range: 800-2,000 Hz fundamental; harmonics extending higher. Frequency range appropriate for forest propagation. Carries 200-400 meters through closed canopy.
acoustic_data.duration: 5-15 minutes of continuous alarm documented. Unusual among raptors which typically alarm in bursts with pauses. The Goshawk's continuous alarm is diagnostically distinctive.
acoustic_data.physical_escalation: Alarm → diving attack from behind → talon contact with back of head or shoulders → multiple simultaneous attacks by mated pair. Head wounds documented. Helmet requirement for nest surveys. The most physically committed nest defense in North American raptors.
acoustic_data.e7_calibration: E=7: sustained without modulation, physical contact accepted, real injury risk to intruder. Equal to Bald Eagle combat (297) by different mechanism: prolonged alarm + physical attack vs. aerial tumble.

nodes.title: First Accipiter Genus in Engine
nodes.description: The Accipiter genus enters the SPS engine at degree 298. Two previous Accipitridae entries (221, 297) documented the sea eagle genus Haliaeetus. Degree 298 introduces Accipiter: short rounded wings, long tail, forest pursuit specialist, fundamentally different ecology and morphology from the soaring sea eagle. The Accipitridae family now documented with two distinct genera.

nodes.title: Most Aggressive Raptor Nest Defense
nodes.description: The Northern Goshawk's willingness to make physical contact with human intruders is unique among North American raptors. Bald Eagles display but rarely contact; Red-tailed Hawks alarm but rarely contact. The Goshawk attacks and makes contact. Head wounds documented. Helmet protocol required. Sustained alarm without pause is similarly unique. E=7 is the correct calibration.

nodes.title: Old-Growth Indicator Species
nodes.description: Northern Goshawk requires large territories of structurally complex old-growth or mature forest. Shares this dependency with Northern Spotted Owl (SPS 4, Strigidae) and Pileated Woodpecker (SPS 54, 108, 300, Picidae). Three old-growth specialists across three families: Strigidae, Accipitridae, Picidae. Northwest Forest Plan Late-Successional Reserves provide primary habitat protection.

cross_references.relationship: First Accipitridae E=5. Haliaeetus genus. Raptor era opens.
Second Accipitridae E=7. Haliaeetus return. Locked-talon combat. Two consecutive Accipitridae both at E=7 at 297-298.
Old-growth indicator species: Strigidae. Shares old-growth dependency with Goshawk (Accipitridae) and Pileated Woodpecker (Picidae).
E=8 + E=7 = E=15. Maximum pairing in return cluster. Unrelenting maximum effort: post-hardcore E=8 + raptor alarm E=7.
